Well, thank you very much for having me. I'm truly an honor to be here and I want to share a little bit about my story. And I can pretty much say it starts January 1st, 1999. That was what I would call my independence day. That was the day I became a copywriter. I had no clients. I had not that much skill, but that was my day of becoming a copywriter. About 12 years before then, I was a mother with a two and a half year old and a one year old looking for a job, part time job with a flexible schedule to be able to work, support my husband's firefighter schedule because we needed money. And so I saw an ad in the paper that had a job for customer service and it said flexible hours. That's all I was looking for. I didn't care what the job was. I needed the flexible hours. Happened to be a wonderful company called Fellows Publishing. They're a young company at the time, a direct mail marketing company, which I knew nothing about. And so I plowed for the job, customer service, and I got it paying eight bucks an hour. But again, they would let me have the flexibility that I needed so that my husband and I could raise our children because we wanted to not only have children, but we wanted to raise our kids ourselves. No babysitters, no daycare. That was our goal from day one. And four children later, all grown up, we made that goal. And copywriting had a lot to do with that. So I get started and customer service, the Fellows Publishing worked there for about six months, got to know about customers, what they wanted. I got to understand a little bit about the business and what direct mail was about. Moved from there to an accounting department and then a marketing department was also there on a financial side, but they were about to start a health group. And they were looking to hire from within, which was great. And so I got to be part of, I had no marketing experience whatsoever, but I talked my way, like I always do with everything else. I just talked my way into getting that job and got to be on a team, on the marketing team to help launch a product called Health and Healing, which became a monster success for the company. And the marketing team consisted of me as the assistant marketing manager and this very energetic, effervescent redhead named Wendy Betts. You may know her now as Wendy Makepeace. But I knew her back in the day. And so Wendy and I, our job was to grow that newsletter as fast as we possibly could because we hit a pulse with that market. There was no newsletter like that at the time, Alternative Health. Dr. Julian Whitaker was the leader. He was wonderful. He was passionate. And we just, we were at the right place at the right time. And so Wendy and I worked on growing it, got over 200,000 subscribers at the time. I think it hit to 500,000 at one point, but when I was working with it, we were hitting 200,000. We were doing mail plans, we were doing the marketing. I'm learning everything as I'm going along. You know, I don't know, some guy. So again, as our marketing team was doing this, again, you can't have marketing without copy. And here was this man named Clayton Makepeace, who was just turning out amazing copy. I mean, he was writing things and we were mailing and it was working. And it just like, it was gold. Everything he was writing was just turning to gold. And it was just awesome for us because now we can grow the company because we had copy to write to. And you know, I'm Clayton Makepeace. I hadn't met him or anything like that at the time. I just assumed he was this big Indian guy, you know, make peace. Oh, you know, so, you know, so I finally got to meet him. And not only was he, is he a brilliant person, as you all know by now, but Denise was not under stating anything that she was saying, the most generous and wonderful person I've ever had the opportunity to meet. And so from there, got to work in marketing, got to grow and, you know, fast forward, I have four kids now and get into a point in my career where I'm a marketing director at the company. The company's pulling me to work more hours. My boss is complaining. I'm not working more hours or staying late. My husband's complaining. I'm not home, you know, and I'm like, well, where the heck am I? If I'm not at work, I'm not at home. Where, you know, where am I? And so I made the decision to say, you know, I think it's time for me to go. And I remember it was October of 1998, I called Clayton on the phone from work and I said, I'm really thinking about leaving, you know, my job. And you know, what do you think about it? Because Clayton, you know, I had had a friendship and I really totally respect what he had to say. And I quote, he said, I can't tell you to leave my best comp, my best client, but when you do, give me a call. I was like, okay, you know, so I ended up leaving the company, asked me to stay till December of 1998 and end up staying January 1st. Like I said, that was my shingle called Clayton and said, I'm a freelancer. He said, get your butt on a plane and come to Florida and meet this client named Martin Weiss. You know, this was year 2000 about to happen and Clayton was working on his investment newsletter, launching a Y2K newsletter and he needed someone to help him with the marketing and said, okay, I'm there, you know, so I was making money, decent money, surprisingly, working off from, you know, beginning, but I wasn't writing copy. I was just doing what I did as far as consulting and marketing, but I really, really wanted to write copy. And so one thing that I did, that did help me know that I like copy was when I was working at health and with the health and healing group, there was an opportunity to write an insert. Are you familiar with what inserts are? You know, it just two to four page copy that's put into, inserted into a newsletter to try to sell a product. Well, the time we were doing, we're trying to grow the product and sell other products while we were selling the newsletter and opportunity came to write an insert. And I said, you know what, I'll do it. I'll do it. I knew nothing about how to do it, but I knew one thing. I read everything that Clayton wrote and I mean, we didn't have AWI at the time. We didn't have these type of seminars. So it was just, you just learned as you went along. And I mean, I'm not kidding. I read everything and one thing out of everything Clayton wrote, whether it's financial or health that I always saw was every time I read his stuff, I go, oh my goodness, he's talking to me. You know, it was, it was just, I'm like, oh, okay. And I'm doing this when I'm reading his copy. I'm like, yeah, yeah, you know, all the way to the order form, you know, and then just doing this. So I was like, wow, that's really cool. I, you know, I like that. So I had a chance to write my first insert. I brought it with me. I found it. A two page insert, my first copywriting project, which I didn't know at the time it was. And at the time, a two, a 2% response, I think was considered pretty good for these kind of inserts. This pulled five, 5% first time around. And all I did was I wrote to my mom, you know, because that's who I thought the market was. It was about ABCs of vitamins. People don't understand vitamins and how do you figure it all out? And I'm like, that's my mom. She doesn't know any of this stuff. So I just wrote it. I started off using Michelle A. You know, as my mom, Michelle Anglade. So I did that. And I just wrote like I was writing a letter to a friend and my mom and I can this case, who is my friend? So it worked. And I thought, man, this is really cool. This is really good. I like this, you know, and then I'm seeing these checks for Clayton. I mean, what, you know, like, how do you do six figures in a quarter? You know, I mean, not even a year. It's like a quarter of a year. And he's doing this and he's living, you know, in Florida at the time and just having a great life. I'm like, that's pretty cool. I'm like, I'm not going to be able to do that. I want to be able to do that. I want to spend more time with my kids. I want to, that's what I want, you know? And so I got to know him, got to, you know, work with him better. He and Wendy end up getting together, getting married and having two awesome kids. And so, but I'm like, okay, this is what I want to do. So when I was doing the Freelance, the marketing with Clayton, I said, I got to do my copyright. I want to do that. So I was calling everybody and their mother that I knew. Fortunately, I was in the industry. So I knew list brokers. I knew, you know, other copywriters. I knew other people like, hey, I'm trying to get into this. And they always say, well, sure, send me your sample. I didn't have a sample. I don't, you know what it's like. You don't have a sample. So I just kept trying to talk to people and just saying, listen, I got something for you. I got great ideas. I got this. And I wasn't getting through until one day when a list broker called me, his name was Dave and he says, hey, Carleen, there's a client of mine who has a male potency product. And he said, and he found out that in his files, over 50% of his customers were African American men. And he was, he wants the list broker to find him a black copywriter. Okay. Black copywriter, right? I mean, wasn't happening. This was a white male market. You know, period. I mean, you can find a woman. I knew one female copywriter, Barbara Harrison. That was it. I knew no other minority. Well, I mean, eventually I got to know Kent, who's Asian, but that was it. Nothing, right? So the list broker was having a challenge. Couldn't find a black copywriter. So he said to his client, would you settle for a half black, half German woman? Yeah. And the guy said, okay, let me talk to her. So long. Okay. So then I get a phone call, I'll talk to this, this client. He tells me, yeah, I really think that if I can get the black perspective on this, because I seem to have a lot of black people on my, on my list and I think this is the way to go with it. And, you know, I really think that could help. I said, well, my husband's black. Yeah, that's really help, you know, you know, and so I'm, and I'm feeding into this. I'm like, yeah, you know, really, you need to have that. By the way, that was all crap. Okay. You'd copywriting is blind. It is not culled. There's no race to it. There's no sex to it. There's, you know, no, it's about connecting with your market. That's all this. But I wasn't going to tell that client that because I need the job. Okay. So simple. I'm like, yeah, yeah, you really do need to get the black perspective on this. Yeah. It's going to really make it work for you. I know. Yeah. That's it. You know, I got the job. Yeah. And I called Clayton going, oh my God, I got my first job. What am I going to do? I'm going to do a lot of work. I'm going to go to the mail potency. I don't have that problem with my husband. Like you'll do fine. Go ahead. Just go for it or whatever. So wrote the package, ended up beating the control and I had my first control. So now who, who, yes, how I said it. So now I was going back to all those people that, you know, like, Hey, I want to write for you. And they kept saying, give me your control. Now my story was, Hey, listen, I know you're really busy. So let me send you my most recent control. I want you can take a look at it. I don't waste your time with all the other stuff, but let me show you what I'm doing right now that is kicking butt. And let's talk. Then the phone started ringing. I started getting my clients and the business started to grow. So it's just one break. It's just doing one thing, you know, it's taking an opportunity to take advantage of what you've got in front of you. Just do it. Just keep going with it. Keep pushing. Keep talking to people. You never know. A list broker gave me my first client as far as copywriting, you know, it was another copywriter. It wasn't another company. It was a list broker because I knew him from years before and he, you know, he was stuck. He needed some help and I helped. So your first break will come from someplace you don't expect. So just give it a chance, you know, just keep going out there and pushing yourself and, and, and, and going for it. So make a long story short. I know it's too late. Sorry. But me in the meantime, I'm working on my own, making a lot of money, enjoying myself, traveling, my husband and I, my kids are growing up. I've got there are four children now. Well, they're all grown up in our children. I have four adults now. And I got them all involved in copywriting as early as I possibly could. You know, whether it was my son taking out the trash, you know, for me every day we got home from school. That story about that, by the way, he was about nine years old and Clay and I were working on a promotion called Forbidden Secrets of Sex and Healing. And that is a story we will tell you the long version later, but I can't do it now in my time. Anyway, my son was a job was to take out the trash every, you know, every day he got from school for my office. He came and then all of a sudden I started noticing that he was bringing his friends over to take the trash out, you know, and then I was like, oh crap, what is he looking at in the trash? I bought a paper shredder very shortly. And so that all my all four of my kids have had worked with me at one point and another. I had to fire three of four, three of them. One of them tried to take over my business. I told her to start her own business. That's that one over there. Yeah. So I want, I also asked my girls to come because I wanted them to hear you to hear a little bit from them during my time about their story because they're very close to where you are now. You know, one of them has already got been doing copyrighting for about seven years now and you know, raising a family. The other one couldn't decide which one she wanted to copyright and designing, but now it's gotten her first break with copyrighting. And so I want to just take a couple of minutes to introduce Milan and Tiara to you and let you hear their story. That's okay with you. Okay. Come on up. Yeah. Come on up. Hello. Hello. Okay. So this is a interesting story because I was fired by my mother several, several times. I wasn't. I was the one. I never got fired. She was the only one. Yeah. And getting fired, that's where I had to learn. I had to grow because a lot of it was not being able to accept crits from copies. She would, anything I would write, the red pen, that red pen all over anything I wrote. This sucks. What are you doing? Clayton did the same thing for me. I'm doing it to you. You know, that's kind of, that's, you know, but that's really what helps you grow. So being able to accept criticism from somebody, not criticism, but crits. Okay. Being able to accept criticism from somebody better than you is really a key, you know, here. But for me, you know, the writer's life changed. I always thought I wanted to be able to work on the beach and, you know, to do those things. But really for me, I had, I got married at 23. I had a baby at 24. So my goal, like my mom's, was to be able to raise my son, myself, and to be able to, you know, to spend time with him and to watch him grow and not to have to be tied down to a nine to five. Copywriting gave me that opportunity to be able to go and to travel with my family as well. We're taking a trip out of the country next month. My husband, my two boys, five and one and a half, you know, it's an awesome thing to be able to do it and, and just, you know, getting, getting your first break, getting your first sample, you know, to now seven years later, being hired as a six figure copywriter. That's pretty awesome. So it can come, you know, and, and I was dead broke when I started, you know, coming out from mom and dad's wing, you know, out of a college, you know, it's tough. It's tough, but we weren't the kind of parents that said, Oh, here's some money. Here you go. No, it's like, no, we struggled. You will too. Yeah. Yeah, exactly. So, but, but the school of hard knocks, I guess, is, is what got, is what got me, you know, to where I am now. And it's always growing and always learning. You never stop when you stop learning, when you stop growing, when you stop reading, there's a problem there and you're not going to be able to make money. I want to be able to make more money in less time. That's my goal to continue, you know, to be able to do that. And so I have awesome teachers, Clayton, who went through your system. I still go through your system. I still pull out your little, your booklet on bullets. I read that all the time. I, you know, it's, it's just, it's constant, you know, reminders and being here is, is an awesome privilege. So thank you. Good job. I'm Tiara. I'm daughter number two as referred to in many email easing that my mother has produced number two. I am 28 years old, you know, contrary to my youthful demeanor, almost 28 years old. Sorry, next month. I've been around direct mail, copywriting my entire life, clearly, you know, my mom being involved from the background and growing businesses from being a direct marketing director and, you know, creative and then getting into the writing. So I've just been around it my entire life. So I went the less traditional route. I was 19 and I was struggling whether or not I wanted to stay in school or not. And I had already been working with my mom. You know, she would get a project and, you know, I was like, I need to have money. She's like, well, you're not getting it for free. So you need to work. And I'm like, okay. So she'd be like, I need you to write a sidebar for me. And I'm like, okay. So I just knew that I needed to get money. So I would just, you know, try to write little things and be all proud about what I wrote. And again, turn it into mom and the red pen came out and just marked it all up. And when she turned it back to this was really good. You know, I could use this, you know, here, just make these few changes. And there was probably two words in there that were mine. And I was like, okay, but I did it. And then what made me feel really good is at the end when she produced her amazing package that was kicking butt, she goes, look, there's your little sidebar. And I was like, oh, I was like, you know, yay. But just kind of always being around it because that's honestly is all that I knew. I didn't realize how important it was, all that experience that she was constantly just giving away for free, like kind of like right now. And I was like, okay, that's nice. But you know, now that I'm older, I'm going back to everything that she was saying throughout all those years. I was sitting by her office, writing little things or listening into, you know, conference calls or whatever discussions with, you know, Clayton over there. And I'm just like, oh, now it's all coming back to me. And I'm like, wow, this is so beneficial. You know, this whole experience is so beneficial because they're just giving away gold. And you know, now I'm at a stage where, you know, I tapped into the background of understanding the business of marketing. I went into copywriting. I'm not 100% with the whole, I wasn't sure because again, I'm in, I got experts here, you know, surrounded by experts. And I'm like, I'm nowhere on their level. I don't know if I could succeed in this. But still not letting it go, still taking everything in. Got into the design perspective, had, you know, exposure to Lori Howler. And I'm like a little, you know, little me, and they're just giving away gems that I'm now using, you know, at this stage in my life. And it's been fantastic. And the point where now I've got, you know, clients and I'm like, I don't even know how I got here, you know, kind of a thing and just overwhelmed by that. But it was just taken in the little, little bits, little bits here and there and not giving up, you know, I stayed safe doing special reports as always my little safety net. I'm like, I can do that. That's information. Nobody's really going to read this, you know, I'm not, I'm not, I'm not worried about anything. To the point where one of those special reports that I had written and I think it was probably sitting at a sample because wonderful claims like you want to write a special report for me. I'm like, I'm not here. You know, I'm all excited about it. You know, once somebody comes up and says, hey, do you think you can do some, you know, I just need like a little blast for this new, this new campaign we're trying to do. We're trying to promote women and, you know, healthy skin and stuff. And I'm like, well, sure, I'll try it. And I wrote it and all those years of listening to my mom and, you know, her talking about Clayton and listening to how they, they stylized things. I actually had the best compliment ever when they called me up and said, hey, we read this. We like your style. You know, it reminds me of how Clayton tries to encourage people to, you know, write how you feel. And I was like, no way. I was like, I did it. And I went to my mom, like, I did it. Somebody read something that I liked, you know, so it's just, there's nothing beats that, but it's taking in what everybody's saying and listening and, and taking it to heart and, and just applying it and just keep digging away at it. And eventually you are going to make a success of yourself, you know, in one way or another, you're going to get your name out there. So that's, that's just my little tip. I'm sorry. One thing with Tiara is she, she went to design route first and she's very, she's very talented. She's a natural designer in that sense. And now she's also doing the copywriting. So I don't know too many writers who can do design. I can't, I can write and I can do stick figures to tell the designer what I'm looking for. But one thing she's trying to grow right now is her copywriting and design business. So right now she's got two active projects going on. One as a designer and one as a copywriter. And that's, that's cool. But again, you can figure out where your niche is and you can work it yourself. The beauty about the whole business and that from our three stories right now is it's, it's a big business, but it's, you know, you can make it whatever you want to, you decide what is your copywriter life? You know, if it's not laying out on the beach, I can't lay out on the beach and write copy because I wouldn't be writing copy. Okay. I'd be on the, in the water. I'd be, you know, not doing what I should be doing, but it's whatever makes it important for you. And then you don't have to be an expert. We're not, I'm not an expert. Like Clayton says, we're perpetual students. We're always learning. So remember that it's never going to be a point where you feel like you're, you're amazing or you've got there because there are many times in a course of one day, I will call Clayton and say, client call me and tell me the package didn't work. I suck. I suck. I suck. I suck. You know, and I'm like going, I'm going to get my job at Walmart, you know, be a greeter. And that's all I need to do. That's going to be fine. And then literally a few hours later, I can call and go, client just call me and says your package is kicking butt. I'm amazing. I'm amazing. You know, so I'm so amazing all in one day. You know, so you have to, you have to find a way to deal with that because you're going to get rejected. You're going to find out that your package didn't beat somebody else. It didn't, it doesn't mean that you're bad. It just means you maybe didn't have the right message going out at that time. It doesn't matter. You just keep doing it. Keep going. If you love this, do it. And if you do it, you're going to be successful in it. The end.
